response:
LEVEL

To find the equilibrium population size we set the rate of change to zero so that dP/dt equals zero. With r equal to 0.1 and K equal to 1000 the growth term becomes 0.1P times one minus P over 1000. The harvesting function h(t) oscillates between 5 and 15 turtles per year because the sine term ranges from negative one to one. At equilibrium the growth must exactly balance the harvest so 0.1P(1 - P/1000) equals 10 + 5sin(pi t/6). This creates a quadratic equation where the solution depends on the specific time t because the harvest varies. If we seek a constant equilibrium P_e independent of time we must use the average harvest which is 10 turtles per year. Solving 0.1P(1 - P/1000) = 10 yields P^2 - 1000P + 100000 = 0. The roots are approximately 112 and 888 turtles representing two possible steady states. The larger value 888 is the stable equilibrium while 112 is unstable.

Now we analyze the scenario where carrying capacity declines linearly as K(t) = 1000 - 3t. The equilibrium condition changes to 0.1P(1 - P/K(t)) = 10 + 5sin(pi t/6). For the population to be sustainable the positive equilibrium solution must remain greater than zero. As K(t) decreases the maximum possible population shrinks and eventually the harvest exceeds the maximum reproductive potential. The maximum growth occurs at P = K/2 giving a rate of rK/4. If the minimum harvest exceeds rK/4 the population will crash to zero. The minimum harvest is 5 while the maximum growth is 0.1(1000 - 3t)/4. Setting 0.1(1000 - 3t)/4 = 5 gives 100 - 0.3t = 20 so 0.3t = 80 and t equals 266.67 years. However the question asks when P_e becomes less than or equal to zero which happens when the carrying capacity itself drops below the threshold needed to support even the minimum harvest.
